risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "participants of a previous randomized, controlled, double-blinded clinical trial" and "The trial included 35 patients... Thirteen of the subjects were treated with R. Astragali (AMW), 11 patients were treated with the compounded formulation... and 11 patients were treated with a placebo." Comment: The paper states the trial was randomized and provides no contradictory indication of a non-random allocation rule. Although the exact randomization method is not described in this paper, the report explicitly identifies the underlying clinical trial as randomized and includes a placebo-controlled, double-blind design, which is sufficient here to support a low-risk judgment under the permissive criteria.

Summary: The paper reports that this was a "randomized, controlled, double-blinded clinical trial" and describes placebo and active treatment groups, but it does not state how the random assignment was concealed from the recruiter/enroller. There is no explicit mention of central allocation, sequentially numbered opaque sealed envelopes, sequentially numbered identical containers, or any other concealment mechanism, so allocation concealment cannot be judged as low risk.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The trial included 35 patients... 13 of the subjects were treated with R. Astragali (AMW), 11 patients were treated with the compounded formulation (AMW + Codonopsis pilosula granule [CPG] + Plastrum testudinis granule [PTG]), and 11 patients were treated with a placebo." and "The placebo was prepared using dextrin and an identical protocol to that used for herb granulation. All granules were administered orally with warm water in the fasted state and treatment was administered for 12 weeks." Comment: The study explicitly states it was a "double-blinded clinical trial" and describes an indistinguishable placebo prepared in the same granulated form and administered identically, indicating participants and personnel were likely blinded. Because the main outcomes were laboratory/clinical hematologic measures, successful blinding would reduce performance bias and knowledge of allocation was unlikely to meaningfully influence outcome assessment in this trial overall.

Quote: "previous randomized, controlled, double-blinded clinical trial" and "The blood samples that were utilized in this study were from participants of a previous randomized, controlled, double-blinded clinical trial". Comment: The outcome assessment here was based on laboratory measurement of blood samples (e.g., RT-PCR, Western blot, haemoglobin/HbF and blood counts), which are objective and measured by assay systems rather than subjective judgement. Although the paper does not explicitly state who was blinded to allocation during outcome assessment, the combination of a double-blind trial and objective lab-based outcomes makes bias from lack of assessor blinding unlikely.

Summary: The report indicates that the same 35 randomized patients were used for the 12-week molecular analyses, with no mention of withdrawals, exclusions, or differential loss to follow-up. It states that "Peripheral blood was drawn from the patients before treatment and 12 weeks after treatment" and that "The sample sizes pertaining to the Western blot analyses for the three groups were similar to those described for previous experiments; that is, 13 patients treated with R. Astragali, 11 patients treated with the compounded formulation, and 11 patients treated with the placebo drug were analyzed during the Western blot analyses." Comment: This suggests essentially complete follow-up for the reported outcomes, or at least no signal of imbalanced or outcome-related attrition that would likely bias the result.
